title: From statistical significance to severity
text: A statistically insignificant estimate does not prove that there was no effect. It only means that the observed evidence was not strong enough to reject the null hypothesis.

title: From statistical significance to severity
text: A statistically insignificant estimate does not prove that there was no effect. It only means that the observed evidence was not strong enough to reject the null hypothesis.

title: From statistical significance to severity
text:
The severity question
Severity analysis asks a different question:

Given the estimate we actually observed, which hypothetical effect sizes would probably have produced a stronger result if they were true?

This allows the evidence to be interpreted as a statement about the range of plausible effect sizes, rather than as a simple significant / insignificant binary.



Severity curve for the observed estimate

title: Beyond Statistical Significance
text:
This website accompanies our paper on the interpretation of statistical significance in follow-on damages cases.
The companion website illustrates why statistical significance should not be interpreted as a binary answer to the question of whether an economically meaningful effect exists, and how severity analysis can help courts assess which effect sizes are still plausible after observing the data.
Download the paper

title: Beyond Statistical Significance
text:
What this companion site adds

A visual explanation of statistical significance.
A visual explanation of statistically insignificant results.
A severity-based interpretation of what can and cannot be inferred from an estimate.
Interactive or animated versions of the key figures from the paper.
